thread_tweets.text:
good coins, i dont think you can have one or the other btw, without memes in 2023 a lot of solana's onchain volumes wouldve been much lower & sol wouldnt have gotten attention as a valid fast, cheap, performant L1 to build on - which is what attracts businesses like cards/kled/avici/pumpcade

L1s can be accurately compared to cities, cities have all different types of businesses that bring revenue & residents to the town, for ex. Vegas has a lot of casinos that bring revenue to the city, they also have real businesses like banks, restaurants, hotels, etc etc, just because casinos exist does not mean that other business owners would not want to be entrepreneurs there, in fact it is actually more attractive for them because there are more existing patrons already there gambling that will spend their money on other things

so i think it all adds to the same pie & the attention is net positive, they are just a trojan horse for other things, @solana aims to be the chain where everyone wants to build products on because the infra is so good that it wouldn't make sense to build anywhere else, doesn't matter if that is @Pumpfun or other things like @JupiterExchange / @MeteoraAG / @kamino /@multisig / @BAXUSco / @phantom / @Raydium / @PlayKintara / @NestUSD / @clawpumptech / @squire_bot / @temporal_xyz / @gachasports / @pumpcade / @AviciMoney / @useKled / @Backpack / @Collector_Crypt / @perps / @jtx_trade / @world_xyz / @WSOP etc etc

Retail investors appear to be rotating out of gold and Bitcoin into semiconductor stocks:

Since April, US gold and Bitcoin ETFs have posted -$12 billion in cumulative outflows.

Over the same period, US semiconductor ETFs have attracted +$20 billion in cumulative inflows.

This trend accelerated in mid-May, with outflows from gold and Bitcoin funds more than tripling.

At the same time, inflows into semiconductor ETFs have doubled.

Meanwhile, the largest US gold-backed ETF, $GLD, is down -13% since the start of April, while the largest Bitcoin ETF, $IBIT, is down -12%.

Over the same period, the semiconductor ETFs, $SOXX and $SMH, are up +81% and +60%, respectively.

Retail is driving markets like never before.

The market's rally is broadening:

The S&P 500 equal-weight ETF, $RSP, has outperformed the S&P 500 ETF, $SPY, by +5.0 percentage points so far in June, on track for its best monthly outperformance since 2009.

That would also mark $RSP's 2nd-best month relative to $SPY in at least 23 years.

This comes as $RSP is up +1.9% month-to-date while $SPY is down -3.1% over the same period.

This week alone, the equal-weight ETF, $RSP, outperformed the S&P 500 ETF, $SPY, by +3.4 percentage points, the largest weekly outperformance since the 2020 pandemic.

As a result, the 52-week correlation between the S&P 500 and the S&P 500 equal-weighted index is down to 0.90, from 0.97 in 2022, the lowest since 2003.

Mega-cap tech leadership is taking a breather.

tweets.text:
The new @Grayscale research has me thinking about where institutional flows go next, if CLARITY actually passes.

Hint: not the cheapest names on the list.

Here's what it argues:

→ Assets like BTC are valued on supply and demand, while protocols like Hyperliquid can now be valued on cash flows – the same way you'd value a business

→ Several of the top revenue protocols trade at roughly 1x annual revenue, including @Pumpfun ($459M), @PancakeSwap ($322M) and @MeteoraAG, while @HyperliquidX leads on revenue at $871M at a 15x multiple

→ Out of 15, 11 are financial apps. Grayscale sees the CLARITY Act passing in the next few weeks as the catalyst that funnels institutional flows into this category

→ The revenue multiples look attractive relative to TradFi comps because crypto protocols run with almost no overhead. A protocol at 9x revenue is cheaper than a software business at 9x would be

If CLARITY lands, the image below basically becomes a shopping list.

tweets.text:
It's hard to compare robotics companies and their valuations to each other. Saying one is cheaper than the other for the most part is extremely difficult to do. Which is what I am seeing in a lot of posts. 

There are really big design differences, like bipedal vs fixed or general vs specialized. Which changes tam

Bipedal is the hardest and a bet on generality. So the tam for this is basically saying "all of human labor" but also it means no real niche. So its a competition for all. 

A fixed robot has a smaller tam but is easier to defend. So we get a premium for bipedal. You can't compare it to a fixed robot. Its why you see Figure trade so much higher. And why you can't really compare figure to the others. 

You have to compare each robotics company to same in class essentially

tweets.text: On this day in 1967, Barclays Bank unveiled the world's first cash machine outside its Enfield branch in north London. Inventor John Shepherd-Barron came up with the idea after arriving one minute too late to his bank on a Saturday and finding the doors locked. He originally planned a six-digit security code, but his wife said she could only remember four digits, and the four-digit PIN became the global standard. The device accepted special vouchers treated with mildly radioactive carbon-14 and dispensed up to £10 at a time. ATMs became one of banking's most profitable channel innovations: by the mid-2000s, the global network exceeded 3 million machines and generated billions in interchange and surcharge fees while letting banks cut branch staff costs by roughly 30%.

The absolute moral chaos inherent in the debate as to whether Elon “killed” millions of people in the global south is precisely why we do not as a society reason in raw consequentialist terms. Because everyone with agency ends up with uncountable moral culpability for virtually every action or inaction. When distant lives or those 5000 years in the future are worth exactly the same as your own, you end up completely paralyzed. 

No one reasons like that - aside from shrimp welfare maximizing polycule members in SF - so we can happily disregard the entire debate. All the gotchas and “by your logics” are simply downstream of the fact that consequentialism is an alien and inhuman philosophy that no one real actually espouses.

tweets.full_text:
Students need to learn how to use AI, but they also need to learn how to think for themselves. So in schools there should be some kinds of work where students are expected if not required to use AI, and others where it's banned, and no mushy middle ground in between.

The key is to be explicit: for each kind of work in each class, is AI use expected, or banned? A school that's not explicit in this way is badly run.

The banned uses of AI will usually have to be prevented. Otherwise students will just cheat. This will change the way a lot of classes work, but there is no alternative.

tweets.text:
I am glad I did my bets on it early, because as much as I love robotics, it’s unfortunately starting to feel like a too obvious and crowded trade.

When every shitty crypto VC suddenly expands into AI and robotics, and every Tier 2 robotics company you’ve never heard of is suddenly raising a multi-billion dollar round, you know it’s time to get more careful.

Which makes the next 18 months extremely predictable:

Valuations will keep detaching completely from fundamentals. Every ex-Tesla, ex-Boston Dynamics and ex-DeepMind engineer will raise a seed round at a Series B price. The talent war will send burn rates to the moon. And 90% of these companies will ship nothing but a polished demo video.

Then the bubble bursts, a brutal consolidation follows, and the “obvious winners” getting funded at ATH valuations today will mostly not be the ones standing in 5 years. The market always overpays for the obvious and ignores the quiet teams actually solving the hard problems.

Everyone is talking about the ChatGPT moment for robotics. But the real money will be made on the Claude moment.

Oil at $70 is pricing in far too much optimism.

The U.S. has already projected weakness. The IRGC knows Trump wants a deal and has little appetite to restart the war.

In the Middle East, kindness and good faith are often interpreted as weakness. That makes the risk of further escalation easy to underestimate.

Imagine if the Strait of Hormuz isn’t fully reopened within the next few weeks to a month. The market will start realizing this isn’t a temporary disruption, and oil should begin trading higher.

Another possibility the market may not be pricing in: if Iran ends up imposing significant transit fees or other costs on shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, exporters would likely try to pass at least some of those added costs on to buyers, putting additional upward pressure on oil prices.

I still think oil sees $80 before it sees $60.

- Grass token holders call on July 7 will answer whether rev goes to token holders/whether they're token-aligned. Story pivoting to "Data Foundation" & investing in Kled further validate the demand for data. Reppo about to validate its decentralized data evaluation network/data pipeline with enterprise clients

2026 will be the best year for open/decentralized ai and privacy

tweets.text:
You can't just reason in the abstract without considering the leverage ratio and the size of STRC.
The 25% downside exists because of 'high' the MSTR leverage is, but that's a separate variable compared to the design of STRC in isolation

You could very well imagine a BTC treasury that caps its leverage at 15%, only has a common stock and a STRC-like product, and it wouldn't even have to pay 12% a year to have holders at $100.
That being said, having a STRF pref is probably better than a STRC pref because it's a much cleaner design

tweets.text:
100% agree. We are entering the era of token engineering. The first wave was “use more AI.” The next wave is “use the right token, on the right model, at the right time, with the right cache.”

Routing, defaults, caching, lean context, visibility, all of that becomes the infra layer for making exponential token growth economically sustainable.

My prediction: the next big optimization wave will be background agents.

Not just smart routing across models, but smart routing across time. A lot of agent work does not need to happen synchronously. Code review, evals, refactors, data extraction, doc updates, security scans, research passes, inbox cleanup, CRM enrichment, test generation, migration plans. Many of these tasks can wait 30 minutes, 2 hours, or overnight.

Today we mostly think in fixed token prices. $ X per million input tokens, $ Y per million output tokens. But compute does not actually have fixed scarcity. GPU capacity has peaks and valleys. Interactive usage spikes during the workday so background workloads can run when capacity is cheaper.

So I think we move from: fixed token price to latency aware token price. Aka Need it now? Pay the realtime price. Can wait an hour? Cheaper. Can wait 24 hours? Much cheaper.

This is already starting with batch APIs, but I think it becomes a much bigger primitive. Agents will decide not only which model to use, but when to run the task.

The future AI stack will optimize across model quality, cache state, latency tolerance, GPU capacity, and business value.

The goal is not fewer tokens per se it's fewer expensive tokens wasted at the wrong time.
